speaker: interviewer
text: Alicia Appleman. You were telling me how, through Benjamin, you found out that the next grave was Milek's grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I knew what happened to him. And I thought that if there was a hero among us, he was one of the greatest. But I had to leave, Bella asked me that I had to leave, because I was spending most of my time taking her son at the cemetery. We were just spending time there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I left Poland, this part of Poland, and I went to Western Poland, because at this point, I decided I would have to leave Europe altogether. And I would probably go to [INAUDIBLE] Israel, to Palestine what was known then. Because we were Zionists, and I could never live on-- on a continent which was considered like a cemetery of my people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still didn't know what happened to the rest of the Jewish people in Europe. My father was a partner in a factory in Bielsko, which was Western Poland. So I thought I would see my father's factory. I went there, and while I was living in Bielsko, I found children on the streets. And the way they looked, they were not ordinary children. The way they walked, the way they were dressed. And I found out they were Jewish children. Some of-- most of them were in concentration camps. Some of them were hidden. And I organized them, about 28 of them, and we opened like a little orphanage. We were helped by the-- the Jews from the Russian army. They helped us get food. I was with them, and this is the place where we children helped each other to trust people again, especially adults. We were healing each other through trust. At that particular time, I was getting a lot of clothing from the Russian Jews. And they were getting them from abandoned homes. And I was selling them to some of the survivors who came from-- from Lodz, from the city of Lodz, L-o-d-z.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day I-- yeah, I was only 15 years old. And I was doing all this business, and selling them these things. And-- and they said, where was I getting clothes from? And I say, the Russians are helping me because I helped them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was just 15 years old. And I showed them my documents that the partisans gave me, and the medal, and everything. And they were just joking and very impressed. But then one day, they came and they bought the stuff from me. And they said that there is a woman in Lodz by the name of Silvia who wants to see me. I was curious. I mean, they told me how brave she was, that she was a hero from the Warsaw ghetto. They build her up, and I fell in love with her before I even knew, because a heroic woman like that was somebody that I would do anything for. So I came to Lodz and she engaged me in her organization, which was Brekha, a smuggling organization that was smuggling Jews out of Poland through Bratislava, Czechoslovakia, Hungary, then to Austria, and then into the occupied area occupied by the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name? Her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Silvia. At the time, I didn't know her last name. I found out her name was Silvia Lobatkin. Only now I know, but to me she was Silvia. And because of these, documents, I was able to go in and buy documents for us, for the people. I had 33 people in-- each time in the group. And at the beginning, I was learning with another girl, but then I was taking them independently. I got-- was so good at smuggling the people out that they gave me deserters, Jewish deserters from the Russian army, and I smuggled them out. And then one time I got ill and I had to stop smuggling. I got problems with my lungs. I had tuberculosis during the war, and I lost the use of one of my lungs. And so I stayed in Austria, in Bad Gastein, in a very nice place which was very good for my lungs. And that was 1945, 1946. And in 1946, those who were in this organization of-- of the Brekha were promised papers to go to Israel. But I had to give up my papers for somebody who was more important, the legal papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were going to Belgium and meet up with the Jewish brigade who fought with the British. Go on their ship to Israel. So before you continue, do you still have your medal [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, I-- I didn't have my medal. What happened-- what I did with my medal and my papers when I got ill, I sent them back to Lodz to be used. Another girl could use them, because they-- it didn't have my picture. Now, my medal and my papers came eventually to Israel with somebody who came to Gush Etzion. But Gush Etzion was overrun by the Jordanians in the war for independence. So I-- I lost that. But if I were now to write to President Yeltsin and tell him, he would look up the history with the Kovpak partisans, he would find my name not under Alicia, but [INAUDIBLE], they gave me a new name. And he would probably give me back the medal and the papers, and probably give me a pension. But you see, I don't have that relationship with the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to your-- continuing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then from Belgium, I was in Belgium in '46, and then I went to a school, to an orphanage, there in [? Mechelen ?]. And in the spring of '47, all the 600 boys and girls, mostly survivors of camps from Czechoslovakia, Hungary-- I was the only Polish girl survivor-- were brought on an illegal ship, were brought to France, put on an illegal ship, called the and we were sailing for Israel in the spring of 1947. Unfortunately, our ship was leaking. And instead of going, like, for six days, we had about-- several weeks of very hard pumping and sailing. We finally got to Israel. We were captured by the British. We fought with the British for two hours. We threw all the garbage we collected for them. But then finally, they threw tear gas into our ship. And they boarded us, and they took their vengeance because quite a few children were thrown overboard, were clubbed over the head. I have pictures of-- of-- of coffins taken off my ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was taken with all of the other people to Cyprus, where I spent eight months in a camp. A youth camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date were you taken there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From March until December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947. In December, Golda Meir did come to Cyprus. And she took 1,000 children out of there, and I was one of the 1,000. And then I came to Israel, and I went to a youth Aliyah school called Mikveh Israel. Mikveh Israel is located between Tel Aviv and Holon. And I was there for my senior high school year to finish my studies, because I was going to school in Belgium, and I was going to school in Cyprus the eight months we were studying a lot. Then the War for Independence started. And while I was in Cyprus, we were trained by the Palmach. And then when I was in Cyprus, we had combat duties. We fought in [INAUDIBLE] and Tel Aviv, and then in the capture of Jaffa. Our class- our whole class of 32 kids went in to fight. We had to get into Jaffa. And luckily we had the [INAUDIBLE] helping because they were very, very well-trained soldiers. And then, I-- after we captured Jaffa, I was moved to the headquarters of the Israeli Navy, and I worked as a [INAUDIBLE] officer in the Israeli Navy for the remaining of my two years in the Israeli Navy. I met an American by the name of Gabriel Appleman, who came to help us save our lives. We were married in 1950. And because his father was ill-- he had a brain tumor operation-- he brought me to the United States in 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what it felt like to be living in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, for me it was very, very important. The war was going on, but you must understand, Cathy, I had the chance to go to the United States because young teenagers were, registered to go and be adopted by some families in the United States. And my friend registered me. But I knew whatever, is going to happen after the war, I knew that the people in-- in Palestine, my people, are going to fight for their, lives. My destiny is with them. And so I knew that this is one time, even though during-- during the war I was able to fight against the Germans in a way I knew how by saving lives, I knew that I will be able to be a combat soldier for my land. And if I have to die, I will die. But I will be able to fight. At least I will have a chance. So I was not afraid at all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the United States to help my father-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came October, 1952. My husband went first. And then I got my papers, and I came October, 1952. It was very cold here. I remember, I came from Israel. It was warm there. And we came for a little while--

speaker: interviewer
text: What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to Brooklyn, to Flatbush. And we thought, we will stay here a few years, help Papa, and then we'll go back to Israel because that was my homeland. But Papa never got well, and my husband was the oldest son, and it was just proper for us to stay. Then I discovered that I had difficulties in having children, because the typhoid that I had and typhus infected the reproductive organs, especially the fallopian tube through which the egg has to travel to get fertilized. And so I started going to a fertility and sterility doctor, to Doctor Weisman in-- in New York. And it took me about five years, and through a miracle, our son Daniel was born on December 11, 1958. And that was just really a miracle. And they had published a whole article in the 'Parade Magazine,' because it was a medical miracle that I had a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have today?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have two sons and a daughter. Daniel, the firstborn, then Zachary-- and-- then Ronnie, and then Zachary. And we have two granddaughters, Maya and Kendra. And Maya is nine, and Kendra is six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you been in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have been in the United States since 1952. From 1969 to 1975, our family lived in Israel, so I was there during the Yom Kippur War. And I'm sorry there was a Yom Kippur war, but I was very glad that I was there, because at least I didn't have to worry myself to death. Being there, I could help and do whatever I could.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- lived in Israel a total of 11 years of my life. And you know, Cathy, I'm like a woman with two husbands. I love Israel passionately, and I love the United States passionately. I love both countries, and I'm very fortunate that they are wonderful friends, because I love both countries very, very much. And what have you-- have you-- how did you finish your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have been like what you would call a perennial student. I always go to school. I love going to school. I have many credits. If I put them all together, I would probably have a couple of doctorates. I've been just for years and years. And I have been a speaker since I promised my brother that if I lived I will tell his story. I have been a speaker since I was able to master this complicated English language. When I was telling my story, it was very early. It was in the '50s and in the '60s. It was very difficult for people to understand what I was saying, but since I promised-- I promised that I will, and I did. And I took my story eventually to the schools to tell it to the children, to the students. They, in turn, urged me each time, saying Alicia, if you could only write this story down, our parents would love to read it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1982, the opportunity came when my husband's company moved us to Holland. So I sat from 1982 to 1985, and I wrote this book called In this book, I recorded all I have told you and much, much more. It has been received with great love by the students from fifth grade to university students, by their parents. I speak all over the country, in Canada. Could I ask you, what did you feel the day you finished writing your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I finished writing my story, I realized something very important. And I said it to my husband. Gabriel, I don't feel guilty anymore for surviving. Many of us-- and I can speak for myself-- not that I could have helped it that I survived, but I always felt that if anybody deserved to survive in my family or was destined to survive, it should have been my brother Zachary and not-- and not I. And so I was-- I felt very guilty. But after I finished writing this book, I realized that even if I didn't survive my brother Zachary still would not have survived, because he chose to fight back. He chose to endanger himself every day. He chose to give his life to save other people. This was his destiny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was-- you know what I said? My God, I was stunned by the things this young girl Alicia did. I said, what a girl. I just cocked my head and I said to my husband-- what a girl. Even I did not realize fully, even telling my story. After three years-- it took me three years to write this book-- I relived every moment of what happened with great intensity, with a lot of tears.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I said, God, there should never, ever be a girl like Alicia. Nobody should ever have to testify to this kind of hell and to this kind of tragedy. But I-- I don't feel guilty but it's certainly a case of catharsis. But what I do feel-- very humble that I was able to be the voice of these very brave children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How has all these experiences affected you in the way you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my children, if you have dirty rooms, I can perhaps understand it. I will close the door. If your grades are not up to A, I will not be very happy, but I can understand. But one thing I shall never, ever tolerate is any remark of people-- about people's religion or people's race. This I shall never tolerate. People are who they are. They are born into communities that they are born, in. They have to be respected. People should be regarded for their own self-worth, for their behavior, and not what religion they belong to. And I must say, with humility, that my children are good people. They care about others, they have been big brothers, they take care of people. And this is all I can really ask from the good Lord.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what message would you like to give your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to tell my children that they have a heritage of a very proud people. That they are what they are. They should be proud who they are. They should communicate with others, give others respect, and make sure that every person at all times in their lives protect other people's human rights. We have to be our brother's keeper. We have to care. We have to care about the little Mexican children that they should have doctor's care, that they should have a good life, no matter how their parents brought them here. They are children who should be protected. Children should be protected at all times anywhere in the world and should be helped, because they are always the first victims whenever a war starts. As to you, dear listener, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want you to know that there were many brave children who maintained their belief in God and their dignity. They died, but they never, ever let the Germans destroy their soul. I would like to have this opportunity to read to you the last message in my book. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] 'I believe that this book will teach young people what enormous reserves of strength they possess within themselves. I pray that all its readers, Jew and non-Jew alike, may unite in the resolve that evil forces will never again be permitted to set one people against another.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much, Cathy. I would like to-- to thank this organization of the Shoah, under the leadership of Mr. Spielberg, for giving us an opportunity to tell our stories in two new generations of the future. I have learned from the past three generations how splendid you are, how understanding you are, how much compassion you are. And I thank you for taking us, the children from this tragic world, into your heart. And I hope the generations of the future will do the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you for your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mrs. Appleman, would you please introduce me to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Gabriel Appleman.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Appleman, what has it been like to be married to a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a-- it's an experience which a few others have had. I-- I-- you know, I-- I-- I was not a survivor myself. I did not live through the Holocaust. I met my wife in Israel when-- during a period where-- where nobody really wanted to talk very much about it. And I don't think I really understood fully just what had happened in Europe until quite a few years later. Of course, it's-- it's-- it's difficult. You know, she used to wake up screaming at night, and I never could understand quite why. And it-- it-- it made it-- I guess, you know, you run into this, gosh, it could have been me sort of psychology.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you like her tell her now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell her now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. It's-- it's a long time. You know, we've been married an awful lot of years. What could I tell her now? That I'm still here for her. And she's trying to help people remember, so that when we're both gone people don't forget about all this, which is important, I suppose, because Most American Jews during World War II didn't really understand what was going on. And-- and I understand that some of our leaders knew, but the great mass didn't. I certainly didn't. We didn't know in the Army, And we didn't know even right immediately after the war. I remember seeing streams and streams of refugees after the fighting ended, and it never occurred to me to wonder whether any of them were Jewish or any of them were not Jewish. And they all spoke strange languages, and they had their pushcarts, and their trucks, and-- and trying to find some place to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and to finish, what would you like to say to her, just in one sentence?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go from strength to strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I say something to him? I want to thank you, Gabriel, for bearing our anguish and for editing my book. And for supporting me now, when I am beginning to travel again. And for just being there for me. Thank you so much. Thank you, Miss Cathy. You are just a wonderful lady. Truly wonderful, sweetheart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are they going to leave this in the tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you for introducing me to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very, very much, dear heart. And I would like to thank Raul. And I would like to thank [INAUDIBLE] and everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in 1985. From right to left is my husband, Gabriel Appleman. And then I am Alicia Appleman. And then there is Daniel Appleman, our son. Our daughter Ronit Appleman, and now she is Rowan Bear. Eric Peterson, and our youngest son, Zachary Appleman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The photo was taken in 1985 in the city of Santa Cruz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our two granddaughters. From right to left is Maya, and the little one is Kendra. This picture was taken in 1993. I call them the barefoot contessas. I made their dresses, and I just really think they look cute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Santa Cruz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names are Maya and Kendra Peterson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] The book, won the-- the 1989 Christopher Award. Christopher Award is given each year to recognize the creative writers, producers, and directors who have achieved artistic excellence in film, books, and television. The highest-- it's affirming the highest values of the human spirit. also won the LYCRA award, the French award for heroism given by the Ministry of Human Action. was translated in eight languages in Europe, and is read all over the world.
